data.title: COLO: Addressing Headwinds And Outperformance Potential
data.description:
Global X MSCI Colombia Outperformance Potential

Colombia's stock market has been one of the top performing emerging markets in recent quarters, and shares still appear very attractively valued.

Colombian equities have broken out strongly, most notably in 2025, and have outperformed emerging markets by over 80 percentage points in the past three years.

Emerging markets will likely face new inflationary pressures and slower growth in 2026-2027 amid rising tensions in the Middle East. Colombia's economy is already facing a lot of economic headwinds, as debt levels have been rising, and the country's Central Bank will have a much more difficult time cutting interest rates. A lot of the country's economic risks have increased since I last wrote a more favorable rating of Colombia in late 2024. Colombia's oil production has also been shaky, and it currently will not enjoy ample benefits from rising oil prices.

The main appeal of Colombia at the moment is that its stock market is attractively priced relative to other emerging markets. Colombia appears positioned to continue to slightly outperform other emerging markets in the coming years. I don't think it makes sense to sell amid rising EM tensions, but I think returns in 2026-2027 will be much more limited.

The Global X MSCI Colombia ETF (COLO) looks like a hold at the moment, as regional and valuation appeals will only slightly offset some of the economic challenges ahead.

Colombia Economic Outlook

Colombia's economic outlook is a mixed bag, as its 2025 growth was largely supported by rising public spending. Public spending rose by 5.8% in 2025, which partially offset other weaker areas like investments and exports. Colombia will likely face a new wave of economic risks this year, including inflation, rising debt, and weaker household consumption. Colombia has failed to attract adequate private investments and to ramp up oil production, which will likely result in slower economic growth.

The latest projections from BBVA call for 2.7% growth in 2026 and then 1.8% growth in 2027. Global growth projections have already fallen by 0.4 percentage points since the onset of tensions in the Middle East.

Rising regulatory requirements in Colombia have caused oil production to decline in Colombia, which underpins its full growth potential. Colombia's economic growth was previously over 10% following its post-COVID recovery and later remained above 7% in 2022. Colombia's economic revisions will likely be similar to those of many other emerging markets, which will experience revised downward growth projections and new inflationary challenges.

Colombia's Annual GDP Growth

Colombia is positioned as one of the region's top 5 oil producers and could benefit from increased export revenue this year if prices remain elevated. Leading players like Ecopetrol SA (EC) and the Argentina based YPF SA (YPF) have massively outperformed US equities in the past month. COLO has exposure to this theme, which should help support its performance this year.

One of the other factors holding back Colombia's growth has been the prolonged high-interest rate environment, which likely won't be reversed in 2026-2027. Colombia's Central Bank will now likely be forced to hike rates slightly in 2026.

Inflation recently declined to 5.3% in February 2026, compared to 5.5% in the previous month. This level is still well above its 3% target and will likely experience further upward pressure as energy supply side risks carry into Q2 2026 and beyond.

Inflation in Colombia

Colombia's Central Bank recently raised rates to 11.25% and will likely be pressured to hike rates slightly in 2026. In the absence of geopolitical tensions in the Middle East, Colombia would have likely been able to keep rates below 10% since inflation improved significantly in 2025.

Another major concern is the rising debt levels in Colombia, which have currently risen above the 2021 highs. S&P Global recently lowered its rating for Colombia to neutral, on par with Mongolia, due to concerns over its rising fiscal deficit, which currently stands at around 6% of GDP.

These economic risks appear manageable at the moment, but there likely won't be strong enough sentiment to support a continued strong rally in the market.

Global X MSCI Colombia ETF

Despite these macro risks approaching and the broader risks that will likely hit emerging markets this year, Colombia's stock market still stands out based on relative value.

Colombia's stock market has begun to catch up with emerging markets following years of underperformance, and it still trades at a notable discount to MSCI emerging markets. However, this is largely due to industry composition, and the MSCI Colombia Index only has three constituents.

A better proxy for the market is the Global X Colombia ETF, which targets a basket of 24 companies in Colombia, providing more reasonable exposure than the MSCI Colombia Index. This is one of the best options, which also invests in some of the leading Colombian companies that have ADR listings.

The Global X Colombia ETF primarily targets companies in lower valued industries like financials, utilities, materials, and energy, to name a few. Regional energy stocks have rallied in recent weeks, and other relatively out of favor themes offer strong diversification from the tech/large-cap driven rallies of the MSCI EM Index and ETFs that track this index.

COLO still trades at a strong discount to the MSCI EM Index, largely because of its industry approach and lower exposure to IT and consumer stocks. Investing in relatively out of favor themes like this in the market may be a less risky strategy as broad based EM indices experience stronger pressure this year.

Colombia still looks like a favorable value play, relative to both emerging markets and its regional peers, and only currently trades at a premium to Brazil. Holding COLO in this environment can also be a suitable strategy for dividend investors, as Colombia currently has a higher yield than both the MSCI Emerging Markets Index and most of its regional peers.

Index PE Forward PE PB Dividend Yield
MSCI Colombia IMI Index 12.51 10.33 1.59 4.82%
MSCI Chile Index 15.19 15.06 1.99 2.46%
MSCI Peru Index 16.91 14.05 3.46 2.54%
MSCI Mexico Index 16.14 12.79 2.42 3.80%
MSCI Brazil Index 10.89 9.82 2.05 5.44%

Source: MSCI latest data ( March 31, 2026)

Colombia and other regional peers currently stand out in the market and have finally begun to receive more interest from foreign investors. This preference will likely carry into 2026 and cause Colombia to outperform the broader MSCI Emerging Markets Index.

Emerging markets will likely begin to face more economic pressure during the second half of this year, as inflation readings pick up, and equities have not really had a strong selling response to recent geopolitical tensions.

The relative value of Colombia's stock market, coupled with the improved outlook for the commodity driven narrative of the region, should provide more support for Colombia as emerging markets potentially face headwinds. However, economic pressure will likely carry into 2026 and 2027, leaving room for limited gains in the market. COLO looks like a good vehicle for dividend investors in emerging markets, but will likely only provide modest capital gains in the upcoming years. Brazil is also another option for emerging market value investors to consider.

data.title: DeFi Development: Better To Buy Solana Directy
data.description:
DeFi Development (DFDV) differs from Strategy (MSTR) in its focus on Solana (SOL-USD) rather than Bitcoin (BTC-USD) as its cryptocurrency of choice for its treasury. This strategy was essentially conceptualized by MSTR and involves tapping the public markets for perpetual liquidity through back-to-back stock offerings to raise cash that's used to buy crypto. DFDV currently holds 2,223,074 SOL, with these currently valued at $184.58 million. DFDV bought its SOL at markedly higher prices than the current level, with its average price per SOL currently sitting at around $159.05. The company's core metrics are not great, with its market price to NAV ratio ("mNAV') sitting at 0.6x, markedly below the 1x benchmark required for DFDV to be able to sustainably tap its equity to buy more SOL. This metric shows how many times DFDV's stock price trades above a SOL per share in USD that's currently at $6.22.

The inverted mNAV implies DFDV is trading at a roughly 40% discount to the value of its SOL holdings. Hence, bulls flag this as an opportunity to acquire SOL for what's essentially 40 cents on the dollar. To be clear, DFDV is trading hands for $3.90 per share against a SOL per share of $6.22. This happened on the back of a stock price that has dipped by 58% over the last 1-year and a 21.2% short interest that has placed SOL in a top 5 position of most shorted small- to mid-cap financial stocks. The bearish thesis here is that the company's ongoing operational layer, in aggregate with its financing costs, and the negative mNAV will create a type of paralysis that prevents the accumulation of new SOL. Bears do face a material risk of being exposed to another cryptocurrency rally that would act to lift the common shares.

Indeed, DFDV's last purchase of SOL was made on the 16th October, 2025, raising the risk of management having to sell SOL if the current inverted zietgiest remains sticky. Such an event would run the risk of clashing with DFDV's mission to grow SOL per share ("SPS"). DFDV's SPS stood at 0.0754 with DFDV having 29,497,394 weighted average shares outstanding as of the end of its fiscal 2025 fourth quarter. DFDV had executed 23 purchases of SOL since its inception, with its first purchase on April 8, 2025, when it acquired 2,858 SOL for $105.24 per SOL. There likely won't be any more near-term purchases until mNAV is at least 1.01x.

Solana, Operational Layer, And Financing Costs

DFDV's cash from operations was negative at $10.93 million for its fourth quarter, a sequential deterioration from cash burn of $5 million in the third quarter. Selling, general, and administrative expenses came in at $6 million. This represents the operational layer of DFDV, the cost of holding the common shares versus just buying SOL directly on a crypto exchange. Hence, a core risk for bulls is not that SOL continues to dip, but that the company's SOL as a treasury strategy becomes fundamentally unviable. SOL forms a base layer for global asset trading and has a healthy developer community. It's a high utility cryptocurrency, and the 7th largest crypto by market capitalization according to CoinMarketCap.

This utility, as a base layer, has not detached SOL from BTC's volatility, with both cryptocurrencies essentially a mirror of each other. Hence, bears raise the question of why buy DFDV when you can just buy MSTR? This means owning a security that's maintaining positive cadence with periodic crypto purchases as it's currently trading at a positive mNAV of 1.09x. The contention now is the viability of the crypto as a treasury business model on the stock market. DFDV's fourth quarter cash burn from operations forms around 11% of its current market cap, with the company ending the quarter with cash and cash equivalents of $9.6 million.

DFDV also had long-term debt of $127.4 million, with this driving around $5.2 million in quarterly interest expenses as of the end of the fourth quarter. This debt, SG&A expense, and cash burn form the headwinds to seeing the mNAV as an opportunity. DFDV is seeing its liquidity base erode, pushing its balance sheet close to a point where it might eventually have to divest its SOL holdings to meet operating and financing costs. The hype cycle for crypto treasury companies has seemingly closed, and like other promising investments before, like the Metaverse, the viability of the business eventually becomes relevant again.

Conclusion

DFDV having to divest SOL at lows could lead to a negative feedback loop where SOL continues to dip because of bearish sentiment cultivated from the first SOL as a treasury company under forced liquidation. This would drive the mNAV lower and force DFDV to have to sell more SOL. DFDV is a levered play on SOL with its debt burden, and this means far too much risk for a crypto currently trading at lows. Hence, investors bullish on SOL would likely be better off if they just own the cryptocurrency directly. I am rating DFDV as a Sell.

data.title: Nebius: The Compute Bottleneck Is Real
data.description:
Thesis Summary

In my last piece on Nebius Group N.V. (NBIS), I argued that this is not just another AI infrastructure play.

It is a strategic asset for Europe and one of the data center plays with the best outlook, combining aggressive growth and balance sheet strength.

That has not changed, and if anything, the last few weeks have reinforced this conviction.

Following a $2 billion strategic investment from Nvidia (NVDA), Nebius has raised $4.3 billion in fresh capital and signed a massive $27 billion deal with Meta Platforms (META).

And the underlying market data is becoming impossible to ignore.

GPU capacity is sold out, and prices are rising. The computer bottleneck is real.

Nebius is one of the few companies positioned to monetize this move at scale.

I maintain a Strong Buy and believe Nebius could be worth well over $200.

Follow The Numbers

One of the biggest mistakes investors are making right now is relying on a narrative that no longer reflects reality.

There’s talk left and right of an AI bubble, but that’s not what the numbers are showing, unless by bubble you mean growing AI demand.

Recent data shows that rental pricing for Nvidia H100 GPUs has surged nearly 40% since late 2025.

Capacity across providers is effectively sold out, and supply could tighten even further.

Nvidia will likely have to delay its Rubin GPU due to a lack of HBM4 supply. We also have reports that Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM) could be reaching capacity limits.

This puts companies like Nebius in a great position.

Nebius Is Scaling Into Scarcity

Nebius is benefitting from this environment while actively scaling into it.

The company recently raised $4.3 billion through a convertible note offering to fund further data center expansion.

This followed the $27 billion agreement with Meta, which provides long-term visibility, increases the backlog, and validates the glaringly obvious fact that AI demand is not slowing down.

The Power Bottleneck Matters, Too

The other key thing investors need to consider is that the compute bottleneck is not just about assembling GPUs; it's about powering them.

In this regard, Nebius is targeting more than 3 gigawatts of contracted power by the end of 2026, with projects spanning Europe and the United States.

This includes a 310-megawatt AI data center in Finland and a planned gigawatt-scale facility in Missouri.

Power is quickly becoming the limiting factor in AI deployment, and that’s why data centers with secured and contracted power are the true bottleneck.

By securing power capacity early and at scale, Nebius is effectively building a moat that is difficult to replicate.

Valuation: $180 Is Too Conservative

In my previous piece, I laid out a $180 price target based on relatively straightforward assumptions.

But I did say then that Nebius should move far beyond that eventually.

Nebius exited 2025 with roughly $1.2B in ARR and is targeting $7B to $9B by the end of 2026.

Applying an 8× multiple on this implies a ~$64B valuation.

We now have clear evidence that compute is not becoming commoditized in any way. If anything, it is becoming more scarce.

GPU pricing is rising, capacity is sold out, and contracts are extending further out.

Demand is expanding, and so should valuation metrics.

If we apply a 10× to 12× ARR, it would give us a valuation range of $80B to $96B, meaning a share price closer to $300.

And that doesn’t even take into account all of Nebius’ related AI businesses, which are also benefiting from this environment.

Risks

In order to justify this valuation, Nebius needs to shatter the already bullish revenue estimates.

This is what the market is slowly pricing in now: a sequence of earnings and revenue beats in the coming quarters.

If Nebius fails to deliver, the downside could come fast.

There is also the risk that supply eventually catches up with demand, particularly as next-generation GPUs ramp.

If that happens faster than expected, pricing power could ease.

And of course, customer concentration remains a risk, with most revenues coming from a few big players in the space.

Final Thoughts

Ultimately, despite the price, I see Nebius as a stock you just want to hold onto and keep accumulating.

If AI really delivers on its promise, then this is a life-changing technology, and Nebius will be one of the key providers of the infrastructure.

The real risk is missing the boat on a once-in-a-lifetime step change in how our economies function.

data.title: Trump Joins Iran, Blocking The Strait of Hormuz
data.description:
Investment Thesis

After the US-Iran peace talks in Pakistan collapsed over the weekend, President Trump announced plans to block Iran's waterways (including the Strait of Hormuz) to put pressure on the Middle Eastern nation. Until now, Iranian vessels, those flying flags of its allies, and tankers that have paid a fee were allowed passage. President Trump said that those will be blocked starting today (April 13, 2026).

Historically, economic sanctions against Iran have been effective and have pushed for concessions that put American and International inspectors in Iran's nuclear facilities, ensuring that enriched uranium doesn't exceed levels enough to create a nuclear weapon. However, it took years to reach that point, and there was also global pressure. Trump withdrew from the Iran nuclear deal in his first term, and equally important, he is spearheading the current efforts with only Israel. For investors, the risk-on trade is still in play.

There are multiple strategies to navigate the current conditions. Investing in oil & gas is one option, but without proper hedging, this trade exposes investors to the exceptionally high volatility of the underlying assets given the current geopolitical situation. In just the past two weeks, WTI oil jumped between $96.6 and $115.6 per barrel, reflecting optimism and subsequent pessimism over the Iran/US deal.

Another smart trade is buying shares in the NEOS NASDAQ-100 (QQQI) fund, a covered call ETF consisting of NASDAQ-100 (NDX) securities plus short call positions aimed at enhancing its monthly dividend distributions. Selling covered calls generates a premium to the seller while giving the buyer an option to buy the NASDAQ-100 index at an agreed-upon strike price. What makes QQQI unique is its concentrated option positions, making it the perfect bet that the Iran hostilities (or their lingering effects on the NASDAQ) extend until at least May 15, 2026, the day when all its concentrated options expire. At the time of this writing, QQQI's call option contracts expired at either 24,300 or 24,800. The NASDAQ now trades at 23,065, having rebounded in recent days on the back of the recent ceasefire deal, yet it is still 5.3% and 7.5% below breakeven, meaning that if the NASDAQ remains at these levels until May 15th, the call options would expire out of the money.

Another strategy is dollar-cost averaging. The current situation won't last forever, and building positions gradually assuages the risks of wrongly timing the market. This isn't a portfolio strategy but instead a trading approach suitable for the current environment, and equally important, it is applicable across different strategies.

Regardless of your investment strategy, the developments over the weekend reinforce the fact that the risk is real. Equally important, I don't think that the market has seen the full impact of Oil & Gas disruptions, particularly in the US, a scenario supported by the difference between the price of physical oil barrels in the Middle East and Oil Futures trading on the markets.

Recent Developments

The US has threatened to block all oil tankers coming out of the Strait of Hormuz, adding further pressure and disruptions to the global energy markets. The situation is very fluid, making it hard for investors to assess the timeframe of the ongoing military operation and its impact on oil & gas flows. Roughly 20% of crude oil and 25% of Liquefied Natural Gas "LNG" exports go through the Strait.

Up till now, Iran has allowed passage for its own tankers and vessels of countries that Iran considers non-hostile, including China and Spain, while implementing a toll fee on other vessels. Thus, the Strait wasn't completely closed but nonetheless operated at a fraction of its pre-war capacity.

While this provided Iran with economic benefits, the US, until today, has been willing to look the other way to support the energy markets. The US now will pursue any tanker that pays the Iran toll, while Iran threatens to target any tanker that doesn't, putting operators between a rock and a hard place.

From a macroeconomics perspective, this means that the risk trade is still one. Also, these dynamics create a complicated situation for oil & gas tanker operators. First, one would expect higher revenue as sea routes are redrawn, most likely on longer lines, which means higher revenue and fewer idle ships. On the other hand, even if new routes are established, there isn't enough spare capacity to compensate for the Gulf region's production. Qatar alone represents ~20% of LNG exports. Spare production capacity from places like Algeria and Nigeria and other safer regions might not be enough to compensate for the gap created in the Gulf, meaning less oil to transport, translating to a negative impact on tanker operators such as Teekay (TNK), TORM (TRMD), Hafnia (HAFN), and others.

More Pain to Come

The impact of the Strait of Hormuz traffic disruption hasn't been fully felt in the US. The market prices determined using WTI are based on the liquid US markets, namely exchanges in Cushing, Oklahoma, the US oil & gas trading hub. However, the closure of the Strait of Hormuz has created illiquid markets. There have been reports of physical oil barrels selling for $170 in Oman and Saudi Arabia. Lack of liquidity impacts price discovery, meaning that the liquid US markets might not reflect the severity of the situation abroad, particularly in the Gulf region.

Also, the impact was assuaged by the tapping of strategic reserves across the world, most recently in Japan. The US also lifted sanctions on Russian oil to the angst of European leaders.

Long-term traders can no longer ignore the rift between the US and its allies. The message from European leaders is that the world order has changed forever, and new trading policies are emphasizing independence from the US. Europe is now bearing the brunt of the Ukraine war financing after the US axed its military aid last year. A more isolated world might mean a weaker US dollar, opening an opportunity for investors in international markets.

In recent comments, the head of the International Energy Agency "IEA" warned that the oil & gas markets are yet to capture the severity of the situation in the Middle East.

Impact on Defense Companies

Despite all the geopolitical tensions, the market isn't showing much love to the aerospace and defense market. Missile arsenals are being depleted, but the argument that this will create a surge in demand hasn't been enough to support the share prices of defense contractors.

Most Defense contractors engaged in the supply of key missile programs have underperformed the index since the hostilities began in early March.

One reason is that sovereign customers are increasingly realizing that the expensive missile systems currently in use are too expensive for today's drone-based warfare, transforming the battlefield into a war of attrition. Iran's Shahed drone engine is made of a modified motorcycle engine and is being downed by missiles costing hundreds of thousands of dollars.

The international customers of the US military complex are also shopping elsewhere. Gulf nations are looking at British and Ukrainian drones and missiles, diversifying their options.

Finally, the rise of the America First movement has had a ripple effect across the world. Canada's prime minister declared that Canada will no longer rely on the US for military supplies. Turkey is also racing to build their missile stockpiles, realizing the rising geopolitical tensions, but has chosen a local procurement approach instead of relying on its NATO allies, including the US.

The days of our military sending 70 cents of every dollar to US are over - Canada's Prime Minister, April 12, 2026

Drone technology and defense startups are set to perform particularly well in the coming years. One company that comes to mind is Kraken Robotics (KRKNF), a Canadian supplier of deep-water drones used in surveillance, mine detection, and countermeasures. Kraken has more than tripled in value since I last covered the ticker last year.

Final Thoughts and Risks

In the current market, I believe that how you invest is as important as what you invest in. Value-cost averaging could be a solution to the uncertainty created by the geopolitical situation in the Middle East. No one knows when the hostilities will end, but what is almost certain is that they will. Gradually building a position in companies you're interested in could prove a winning trade. Instead of using all your cash in one trade, this strategy would keep you an active participant in current market conditions, and equally important, decrease the risk of making one single badly timed trade.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: I Still Rate EZCORP A Strong Buy, But For A Different Reason Now
data.description:
Investment Thesis

In my previous article on EZCORP, Inc. (EZPW), I leaned hard into the gold and macro argument. I still think that piece holds. But this time, I would like to add something different and, in my view, more important. EZCORP is no longer just a pawn operator that is benefiting from a favorable backdrop. In fact, it is becoming a larger, denser, and smarter pawn platform. And I do not think the market has fully caught up to that shift. The 1Q 2026 earnings report was already strong before I even factored in the January acquisitions. Revenue increased by 19% to $382Mn, while gross profit increased by 20% to $223Mn. On the other side, pawn loans outstanding increased by 14% to $314.4Mn, while adjusted EBITDA rose 36% to $70.3Mn. I also note that the company closed two deals that took the company to 1500 stores across 16 countries.

What I underplayed in the last article was the compounding effect of scale. Back then, my case was mostly about bigger loans, stronger collateral values, and a helpful macro setup. Now, I believe that the market is still staring at the shiny object, literally gold, while the real upgrade sits underneath the hood. The CEO, Lachlan Given, said that SMG adds "immediate earnings accretion" and also provides "an exciting platform for future growth." Based on that, my takeaway is that EZCORP is improving its own economics through footprint, density, better underwriting, and more ways to source customers. And in my view, that is a stronger thesis than relying on one macro variable to do all the heavy lifting.

Since my last coverage, the stock has appreciated more than 75% as of writing this.

Business Overview

EZCORP still does the basic thing at the counter. It serves consumers who are cash- and credit-constrained and makes non-recourse pawn loans. And sells pre-owned merchandise through its stores. But the reason I am reiterating a Strong Buy on EZPW is that this is not some fragile one-quarter trade. The FDIC said that 4.2% of US households, or 5.6Mn households, lacked a bank or credit union account in 2023. This is while EZPW itself describes its customer base as people who need immediate, no-obligation access to cash. And that tells me that pawn remains part of the real financing plumbing for a slice of the consumer base even before one layers in cyclical stress. As of December 2025, EZPW had 547 US stores and 836 Latin American stores. And after the January acquisitions, the number became 1500 stores across 16 countries. I believe that once a business gets to that kind of footprint, fixed capabilities like analytics, procurement, marketing, and integration start spreading across a much larger earnings base.

The Growth Drivers

The big change is Founders One and SMG. EZCORP acquired an 87.7% controlling interest in Founders One in January 2026. And this gives it control of SMG. SMG is a platform with 105 stores across 12 countries. The deal was structured as $64.4Mn made up of the conversion of EZPW's existing $45Mn preferred equity investment, $10Mn of notes receivable, and another $9.4Mn of cash. Now this is important because it tells me that management did not just wake up one morning and overpay for a shiny asset. I believe it likely already spent years inside the opportunity as an investor. SMG generated revenue worth $127Mn and a gross profit of $66Mn. I believe the acquisition broadens the total addressable market (TAM) because Puerto Rico stores also offer auto pawn and auto title loans. This gives EZPW exposure to a higher-ticket secured lending category. And that is the sort of adjacent growth angle that I had not really focused on before.

What makes the SMG deal interesting is not only the store count; it is the quality of the optionality. I note that Puerto Rico is probably the most significant opportunity inside SMG right now. And I say this because this is still the team that built Value Pawn in Florida largely through de novos before selling that chain to EZCORP years ago. In simple terms, I believe that EZPW did not just buy stores; it bought a management relationship, a de novo capability, and a set of new markets it can compound into over time. And that makes this story look more like a consolidator building a long middle game than a company just taking a lucky opening move.

The El Bufalo acquisition looks smaller. But I would not shrug it off. EZCORP paid $27.5Mn for 12 stores in Texas. Now Texas is already the company's largest and most attractive domestic region. According to the Census Bureau, Texas increased its population by 8.8% in April 2020 to $31.7Mn in July 2025. I am emphasizing the Texas population because when it comes to the pawn business, density matters. It means more stores in one market can improve brand recognition, staffing flexibility, local market efficiency, and inventory movement.

It would not be fair if I did not speak about the digital funnel. And this is where the story gets more interesting. In August 2025, EZPAWN launched an online shop in the greater San Antonio area that lets shoppers browse local inventory by zip code. They are also able to see photos and descriptions and then buy in store. A few months later, in December 2025, the company rolled out its Instant Quote tool across all US EZPAWN stores. This gives customers a quote range on 15 categories of electronics in seconds before they even walk into a store. I am saying this because pawn has historically been a physical, walk-in, and neighborhood kind of business. Then now you have a tool that can build a digital top of funnel. They make it easier to source items, easier to discover inventory, and easier to bring customers into the ecosystem before the counter interaction even begins. And I believe that a better funnel usually means better inventory out. That tends to show up later in turns, mixes, and the company's margins.

Valuation

Valuation is not as cheap as it looked when I initiated coverage. Let me be honest about that. The stock has increased by over 83% in the last year. But even after that move, I still do not think the stock is expensive relative to what the business has become. When I look at the multiples now, I still do not see major changes. And I am saying this because, in the last article, the company was at a C+ valuation grade. As of writing this, EZCORP has a C Valuation grade.

The company has its FWD non-GAAP P/E at 15.51x, while the sector median stands at 10.49x. That is below FirstCash (FCFS), which sits at about 19.2x, and a touch below World Acceptance (WRLD), near 21.05x. EZPW is trading above other names such as PROG (PRG) and Atlanticus (ATLC) that sit in somewhat different corners of consumer finance. On TTM GAAP PEG, EZPW stands at 0.48x, which is below the sector median of 0.59x. This implies an 18.5% discount.

When I look at the FWD EV/EBITDA, the story is similar. EZCORP trades at 8.48x, while the sector median trades at 9.01x. This implies a 6% discount. FCFS has its EV/EBITDA at 16.5x, while WRLD stands at 13x. On the other hand, PRG sits at a much lower level, at about 3.7x. I do not think raw multiple gaps alone tell the whole story. And this is because these businesses are not clones of each other. But the shape of the comparison still matters. The closest pawn peer, FCFS, commands a materially higher valuation, while EZPW is one with the more obvious current acquisition cadence and the sharper geographic expansion story. What does this mean exactly? It means that my inference is that EZCORP still has rerating room as the market starts treating it as a scaled consolidator with digital and underwriting upside. Rather than only as a gold-sensitive pawn name.

Key Risks

The biggest risk I see for EZCORP is integration. And this is because the company is trying to integrate the business properly so the ROIC actually shows up for shareholders. EZCORP has to fold SMG into a public company control environment, support all its functions, and still preserve the growth playbook that made the asset attractive in the first place. And if integration drags, expenses could rise before synergies in execution and scale show through.

Final Thoughts

At the end of the day, I still like the gold tailwind. And I still like the defensive demand profile. But that is not why I am reiterating my Strong Buy rating on this piece. I am reiterating EZCORP because the company is bigger, digitally smarter, more acquisition-capable, and more analytically disciplined than the one I wrote about last time. The company has more stores and a better funnel. A better lending and better mix. And that combination is what I believe will support revenue, margin, and earnings growth over the next 12 to 18 months. Even if Gold stops doing all the heavy lifting. And that is why I am reiterating EZCORP with a Strong Buy Rating.

data.title: TOYO: Strong Execution Meets Rising Risk Layers (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
My last coverage of TOYO Co., Ltd. (TOYO) about a year ago (June 2025), with a Buy rating at $3.56, has delivered 226%, as TOYO now trades around $11.63. At the time, I upgraded TOYO from a prior Hold on the back of an operational turnaround that I argued the market was misreading.

I am leaning more cautious at this time following the second half and full year 2025 results released two weeks ago. I believe the investment case for TOYO has evolved. And in this piece, I’ll point out where I believe it has strengthened and where it has not.

TOYO Co. Ltd. - Company Overview

A brief overview for readers and investors in solar equities who may not be familiar with what the company is all about, as it can still be somewhat regarded as a newly listed company. TOYO got listed on Nasdaq about two years ago (July 2024) and describes itself as a vertically integrated solar solution company. TOYO's stated ambition is to become a full-service solar solutions provider spanning the entire solar value chain. By being vertically integrated, TOYO controls multiple stages of the production chain rather than specializing in just one aspect. TOYO’s operations span solar cell manufacturing in the upstream production of wafers and silicon, in the midstream production of solar cells, up to the downstream production of photovoltaic (PV) modules, and now they run a U.S. distribution.

TOYO manufactures solar cells and owns facilities in Vietnam (2 GW capacity) and Ethiopia (4 GW capacity) and also operates a 567,140-square-foot facility module production facility in Houston, Texas. The U.S. facility is targeting 6.5 GW in annual module capacity by 2029.

TOYO sells solar cells primarily through its affiliate Vietnam Sunergy Joint Stock Company (VSUN Co.) and a growing group of third-party module manufacturers. This detail of how much sales mix comes from the affiliate and the third parties is important to track the company’s true sales momentum. We’ll get to the revenue mix part down the line in this piece.

Penetrating the U.S. downstream segment has been one of TOYO's strategic priorities and was one of the main rerating arguments in my previous coverage. TOYO acquired VSUN’s trademarks for $340k across 12 jurisdictions (which includes the U.S.) last September to consolidate its U.S. market identity through the VSUN brand and accelerate its entry into the U.S. downstream module market. VSUN already enjoys a formidable brand presence in the U.S. and has shipped over 8 GW of modules to the U.S. over the years, which gives TOYO immediate bankability.

The acquisition of the VSUN trademarks is the single biggest strategic unlock toward that downstream market penetration for TOYO in the U.S., and one of the main variables I am watching for moving forward. Last year in my June piece, the main catalyst I was watching was the ramp from the Ethiopia facility and the entry into the U.S. module market. Ethiopia has been a huge success, delivering 4.5 GW in cell shipments in FY25 and was responsible for the 641% YoY revenue surge in H2 and the full-year margin expansion to 22.5%. The VSUN brand and trademark acquisition has proved to be the other catalyst too. These have effectively been the catalysts that pushed the first leg of the rally up to 226% since last June. I believe the next leg of a potential rally will depend on TOYO proving they can convert their Houston facility into a branded solutions business and taking advantage of U.S. incentives to boost margins, while navigating a handful of regulatory scrutiny.

There are regulatory headwinds that pose direct risks to TOYO’s U.S. ambitions, some of which are still pending investigations and hearings. TOYO’s whole setup has been built around expanding into the U.S., and the company has tried to stay fully compliant, but that does not mean it gets an easy pass. If anything, the rules seem to be getting stricter as we are seeing protectionism in the U.S. solar market ramp up to protect local players.

Before getting into those details, which are part of what is driving the downgrade in this piece along with some weak spots in the financials, it helps to first look at TOYO’s performance in H2 and full-year FY25, based on recently released results.

TOYO FY25 Earnings Review

TOYO’s FY25 results confirmed that TOYO’s vertical integration and compliance strategies are yielding operational results while also amplifying risks that were either absent or understated in the previous coverage.

Full-year 2025 revenue of $427.4 million exceeded management's revised guidance range of $375 million to $400 million by a meaningful margin and was a growth of 142% YoY. Gross profit increased 340% to $96.3 million YoY. Full-year gross margin saw expansion from 12.4% in FY24 to 22.5% in FY25. Adjusted net income came in at $52.2 million, over 700% up from the $6 million adjusted income for FY24. While GAAP net income was $37.2 million, which is about $15 million lower than adjusted net income. The difference was largely related to non-cash adjustments, including a share-based compensation charge of ~$13.7 million, fair value adjustments of $1.3 million (earnout shares related to the Blue World SPAC merger back in 2024), and a small cash but non-recurring listing expense of about $400k.

The full-year gross margin of 22.5% was a weighted average based on revenue of 16.6% (on $139.1 million revenue) in H1 and 25.4% (on $288.3 million revenue) in H2, meaning the full-year 22.5% was boosted mainly in H2, which lines up with when the Ethiopia facility reached full 4 GW capacity.

Non-GAAP EBITDA came in at $95.8 million, up 40% YoY. Management tied this to better operating efficiency and a shift toward higher-margin, tariff-compliant supply chains, which fits with the margin step-up seen in H2.

The top-line revenue composition shows TOYO is slowly moving from dependence on its affiliate to third parties for sales, and the Ethiopia facility has not just made sales ramp up but given Toyo the leverage to sell directly to a broader North American client base. One of the main metrics I was tracking in FY25 was the concentration of sales from related parties (mainly VSUN), where they accounted for 72% of sales out of $176.9 million total sales in FY24. But FY25 has shown 60% of total sales ($256.3 million) now coming from third parties while 40% of total sales ($171.1 million) from related parties. Though the related party figure is still high in my view and poses risks of contagion, especially going by the fact that those related party sales flow mainly through VSUN, and VSUN is currently navigating intense U.S. regulatory scrutiny. That could slow cash flows and, in turn, delay TOYO’s receivables or even tie up inventory. But a more workable strategy would be that VSUN pushes non-U.S. orders from Asia while Toyo directly services the North American market to circumvent those regulatory hurdles. Even with that strategy, it is worth noting that TOYO is also not fully in the clear when it comes to current U.S. regulatory pressure.

The Ethiopia manufacturing plant was the main differentiator for TOYO in FY25. The better revenue composition, better unit economics that led to higher gross margins, operating expenses as a percentage of revenue actually declining, and even a stronger GAAP bottom line are all tied to the production ramp at the facility.

Via that facility, TOYO has been able to avoid specific duties because Ethiopia is not included in the current wave of U.S. AD/CVD (Anti-Dumping and Countervailing Duties) investigations that target Southeast Asian countries. TOYO took it a step further by recently securing a sales contract with a U.S. polysilicon manufacturer to secure polysilicon supply for its solar modules, making the Houston facility tilt more towards domestically sourced materials. I believe TOYO’s approach in covering all angles of compliance to stay eligible for the U.S. market explains why its unit economics in FY25 appear of more quality than in FY24.

This setup also explains why TOYO’s unit economics appear of higher quality than that of a peer like JinkoSolar (JKS), who have been hamstrung by these duties, despite having a much higher headline sales volume in GW. JKS’s gross loss margin was negative 2.5% in Q1, before improving to a 2.9% gross margin in Q2 and 7.3% in Q3. JinkoSolar has invested heavily in Vietnam (including a $500M wafer plant) to serve the U.S. market. However, in June last year, when the U.S. Department of Commerce’s AD/CVD duties were finalized on Vietnamese solar cells, that became the precedent for an unworkable cost structure for JinkoSolar, leading to an eventual termination of a planned 4 GW production line in Vietnam early this month, with the key factor behind the termination of that project being the worsening economics of exporting Vietnam-made PV products to the U.S.
